FULL CONTENT:
# Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models

We introduce the AIDA-TNG project, a suite of cosmological magnetohydrodynamic simulations that simultaneously model galaxy formation and different variations of the underlying dark matter model. We consider the standard cold dark matter model and five variations, including three warm dark matter scenarios and two self-interacting models with constant or velocity-dependent cross-section. In each model, we simulate two cosmological boxes of 51.7 and 110.7 Mpc on a side, with the same initial conditions as TNG50 and TNG100, and combine the variations in the physics of dark matter with the fiducial IllustrisTNG galaxy formation model. The AIDA-TNG runs are thus ideal for studying the simultaneous effect of baryons and alternative dark matter models on observable properties of galaxies and large-scale structures. We resolve haloes in the range between 108superscript10810^{8}10 start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 8 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and 4×10144superscript10144\times 10^{14}\,4 × 10 start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 14 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPTM⊙ and scales down to the nominal resolution of 570 pc in the highest resolution runs. This work presents the first results on statistical quantities such as the halo mass function and the matter power spectrum; we quantify the modification in the number of haloes and the power on scales smaller than 1 Mpc, due to the combination of baryonic and dark matter physics. Despite being calibrated on cold dark matter, we find that the TNG galaxy formation model can produce a realistic galaxy population in all scenarios. The stellar and gas mass fraction, stellar mass function, black hole mass as a function of stellar mass and star formation rate density are very similar in all dark matter models, with some deviations only in the most extreme warm dark matter model. Finally, we also quantify changes in halo structure due to warm and self-interacting dark matter, which appear in the density profiles, concentration-mass relation and galaxy sizes.

###### Key Words.:

## 1 Introduction

One of the foundations of the concordance cosmological model is that approximately 85 per cent of the matter content of the Universe is in the form of some yet unknown component that we can detect only through its gravitational effect: dark matter. Starting from small density perturbations in the early universe, dark matter leads the gravitational collapse on large scales, forming the cosmic-web structure of filaments along which gas flows to the potential wells of dark matter haloes, where it cools and creates stars – eventually leading to the formation of galaxies. Despite decades of experimental searches, the favoured particle candidates for cold dark matter (CDM) – e.g. axions and weakly interacting massive particles (WIMPS) – have so far not been detected. This has motivated the study of alternative models that are allowed by particle physics assumptions and span a wide range of masses – from the eV to the GeV scale.

Moreover, while the CDM model is very successful at explaining the large-scale-structure distribution of the universe (Planck Collaboration et al., [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib80)), it has been challenged by observations at the scale of galaxies and below (Bullock & Boylan-Kolchin, [2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib19)). In the past few years, inconsistencies between CDM numerical simulations and observations that led to the famous *missing satellites* problem (Klypin et al., [1999](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib48)) have been mitigated with the inclusion of baryonic effects in simulations (Brooks et al., [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib17)) and with the discovery of new faint satellites in the Milky Way (Bechtol et al., [2015](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib7); Drlica-Wagner et al., [2015](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib31); Kim et al., [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib47)). However, other possible tensions have arisen, such as the *core-cusp* and *too-big-to-fail* problems (Boylan-Kolchin et al., [2011](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib16)), or the diversity of rotation curves (Kaplinghat et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib46)). Recent observational analyses have also identified new challenges for CDM. For example, the lensing properties of subhaloes appear inconsistent with CDM both at galaxy (Minor et al., [2016](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib64); Ballard et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib6); Despali et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib25); Enzi et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib35)) and cluster scales (Meneghetti et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib62); Ragagnin et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib83); Meneghetti et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib61)): in both cases, the observed lensing signal is inferred to be stronger than predicted, and the subhalo concentrations required to explain it are outliers within the CDM model. It is thus still uncertain if simulated CDM predictions can be brought completely in agreement with observations by modifications of baryonic feedback and other aspects of the subgrid physics in simulations, or if these discrepancies point towards new physics in the dark sector.

Many previous works have addressed the aforementioned tensions, developing a theoretical and computational framework especially suited for warm (WDM) and self-interacting (SIDM) alternative dark matter models. These have been originally motivated by models of the satellite population of galaxies (for WDM), the profiles of dwarf galaxies and merging clusters (for SIDM). While these are certainly among the most relevant effects, the most stringent constraints on the WDM particle mass are derived from observations of the Lyman-α𝛼\alphaitalic\_α forest (Iršič et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib43)) and flux-ratio anomalies of lensed quasars (Gilman et al., [2020a](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib37)), while the self-interaction cross-section is strongly constrained at the scale of massive elliptical galaxies and galaxy clusters (Meneghetti et al., [2001](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib63); Peter et al., [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib76); Despali et al., [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib27), [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib29); Eckert et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib32); Shen et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib99); Mastromarino et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib60)). In general, any dark matter model that aspires to explain observations should be challenged with reproducing the halo and galaxy properties spanning many decades in mass and not only in a narrow range of scales. This is the fundamental aim of the AIDA-TNG project.

The majority of related work is based on zoom-in simulations of limited samples of objects (Zavala et al., [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib124); Rocha et al., [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib90); Fry et al., [2015](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib36); Robles et al., [2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib89); Creasey et al., [2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib23); Despali et al., [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib27), [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib29); Bhattacharyya et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib12); Nadler et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib67); O’Neil et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib74); Ragagnin et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib84)). These can have the advantage of high spatial and mass resolution, but lack the required statistics to test models on multiple scales and fully understand potential biases in the sample selection. At the same time, the inclusion of baryonic physics is fundamental for studying small scales, but only a limited number of available alternative dark matter (ADM) hydrodynamical simulations are of cosmological scale (see Fig. [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S2.F1 "Figure 1 ‣ 2 The AIDA simulations ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models")). For WDM, Oman et al. ([2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib73)) recently presented two cosmological runs that use the EAGLE galaxy formation model: one 25 Mpc box where the WDM model considers a 1.5 keV thermal relic candidate and one 34 Mpc box reproducing the effect of 7.1 keV sterile neutrinos. In SIDM, the EAGLE-SIDM (Robertson et al., [2018b](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib88), [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib87)) and BAHAMAS-SIDM (Robertson et al., [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib86)) simulations have been the first to include both self-interactions and baryonic physics on cosmological scales. This set includes elastic self-interactions with constant cross-sections σ/mχ=[0.1,1]⁢cm2⁢g−1𝜎subscript𝑚𝜒0.11superscriptcm2superscriptg1\sigma/m\_{\chi}=[0.1,1]\,{\rm cm^{2}g^{-1}}italic\_σ / italic\_m start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_χ end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT = [ 0.1 , 1 ] roman\_cm start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman\_g start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - 1 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and a model with a mild velocity dependence peaking at σ/mχ=3⁢cm2⁢g−1𝜎subscript𝑚𝜒3superscriptcm2superscriptg1\sigma/m\_{\chi}=3\,{\rm cm^{2}g^{-1}}italic\_σ / italic\_m start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_χ end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 3 roman\_cm start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman\_g start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - 1 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT. More recently, the TANGO-SIDM simulations (Correa et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib22), [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib20)) explored velocity-dependent SIDM models in 25 Mpc volumes, including the SWIFT-EAGLE physics model (Schaller et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib94); Bahé et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib5); Borrow et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib14)). Finally, the THESAN-HR (Shen et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib98)) simulations include different dark matter variations; however, given their focus on the epoch of reionisation they only follow a ∼similar-to\sim∼6 Mpc box until z=6𝑧6z=6italic\_z = 6.

The AIDA-TNG project advances the field of ADM simulations by providing a consistent set of high-resolution cosmological boxes simulated with the same galaxy formation model and multiple WDM and SIDM models, in addition to CDM. We provide a large simulated sample of galaxies and break the degeneracy between baryonic and dark matter effects at small scales. In this work, we present simulations evolving two cosmological volumes of 51.7 and 110.7 Mpc on a side, evolved with the AREPO code (Springel, [2010](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib103)) in different dark matter scenarios and the TNG galaxy formation model (Weinberger et al., [2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib119); Pillepich et al., [2018b](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib79)).

In this work, we present the first results from the AIDA-TNG suite, providing an overview of the multiple effects of alternative dark matter models. In Sect. [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S2 "2 The AIDA simulations ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models"), we describe the set-up of the AIDA simulations and discuss the considered dark matter models and their properties. We then present our main results in Sect. [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S3 "3 Halo and stellar mass functions ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models") to [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S6 "6 Matter power spectrum ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models"). We start by discussing the halo and stellar mass function in Sect. [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S3 "3 Halo and stellar mass functions ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models") and their evolution with redshift. Sect. [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S4 "4 The structure of dark matter haloes ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models") focuses instead on the ADM effects on the distribution of dark matter and baryons within haloes, exploring the density profiles and concentration-mass relation. We then look at the properties of galaxies in Sect. [5](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S5 "5 Galaxy properties in ADM models ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models"). Finally, Sect. [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S6 "6 Matter power spectrum ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models") presents measurements of the matter power spectra of the different mass components and their evolution in time. In Sect. [7](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S7 "7 Summary and conclusions ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models"), we summarise our results and present our conclusions.

The AIDA-TNG simulations, *Alternative Dark Matter in the TNG universe* (AIDA for short, hereafter), consist of three cosmological volumes of increasing resolution and decreasing size simulated in cold and alternative dark matter models, with and without baryonic physics. In this work, we present the two largest boxes of 110.7 and 51.7 Mpc on a side and the first results obtained from their analysis, while a smaller (and higher resolution) 20 Mpc box will be introduced in an upcoming work. The uniqueness of the AIDA set lies in the fact that we run the same cosmological volumes in multiple dark matter scenarios, spanning cold, warm and self-interacting dark matter. Moreover, we create both a dark-matter-only (DMO) and a full-physics (FP) version of each run so that we can disentangle the effects of ADM and baryonic physics as described by the IllustrisTNG galaxy formation model (TNG hereafter Weinberger et al., [2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib119); Pillepich et al., [2018b](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib79)). We discuss the considered dark matter scenarios and the integration with the TNG model in more detail in Sects. [2.1](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S2.SS1 "2.1 Warm dark matter models ‣ 2 The AIDA simulations ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models"), [2.2](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S2.SS2 "2.2 Self-interacting dark matter models ‣ 2 The AIDA simulations ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models") and [2.3](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#S2.SS3 "2.3 TNG galaxies in alternative dark matter ‣ 2 The AIDA simulations ‣ Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models").

The initial conditions (ICs) have been generated with N-GenIC code (Springel, [2005](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib102)) by applying the Zel’dovich approximation on a glass distribution of particles with a linear matter transfer function computed using the CAMB code (Lewis et al., [2000](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib51)). The 110.7 and 51.7 Mpc boxes (corresponding to 75 and 35 h−1⁢Mpcsuperscriptℎ1Mpch^{-1}{\rm Mpc}italic\_h start\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - 1 end\_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman\_Mpc, respectively) are run from the same ICs of the corresponding runs of the original IllustrisTNG simulations (Pillepich et al., [2018a](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib77)). For each case, we simulate two resolution levels: “A” corresponds to the TNG100-2 and TNG50-2 runs, while “B” corresponds to the TNG100-3 and TNG50-3 runs. Therefore, we can compare to many previous results on galaxy formation in CDM and better understand systematics. Haloes and subhaloes are identified using the SUBFIND algorithm, producing catalogues of the halo and galaxy properties with the same information available for the TNG runs (Nelson et al., [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib72)). ### 2.1 Warm dark matter models

In Warm Dark Matter (WDM) models, dark matter particles are lighter (keV) than CDM candidates (GeV) and thus become non-relativistic at sufficiently late times so as to suppress, by free streaming, the formation of low-mass galactic-scale haloes with respect to CDM. The number of satellites of the Milky Way and the properties of its stellar streams, strong gravitational lensing and the Lyman-α𝛼\alphaitalic\_α forest are all well-known probes of the WDM particle mass mWDMsubscript𝑚WDMm\_{\rm WDM}italic\_m start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_WDM end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Each of these is sensitive to the number of low-mass haloes and subhaloes in certain redshift and halo mass ranges: our Galaxy, gravitational lenses at distances 0.1≤zlens≤30.1subscript𝑧lens30.1\leq z\_{\rm lens}\leq 30.1 ≤ italic\_z start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman\_lens end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≤ 3, and the distribution of neutral gas on large scales. Joint constraints that combine the results of such methods are thus more robust than individual measurements. Very warm models are currently already excluded, with upper limits at m≥WDM5keV{}\_{\rm WDM}\geq 5\,{\rm keV}start\_FLOATSUBSCRIPT roman\_WDM end\_FLOATSUBSCRIPT ≥ 5 roman\_keV (Gilman et al., [2020b](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib38)), m≥WDM6.046keV{}\_{\rm WDM}\geq 6.046\,{\rm keV}start\_FLOATSUBSCRIPT roman\_WDM end\_FLOATSUBSCRIPT ≥ 6.046 roman\_keV (Enzi et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib34)), m≥WDM7.4keV{}\_{\rm WDM}\geq 7.4\,{\rm keV}start\_FLOATSUBSCRIPT roman\_WDM end\_FLOATSUBSCRIPT ≥ 7.4 roman\_keV (Nadler et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib66)), m≥WDM4.1keV{}\_{\rm WDM}\geq 4.1\,{\rm keV}start\_FLOATSUBSCRIPT roman\_WDM end\_FLOATSUBSCRIPT ≥ 4.1 roman\_keV (Iršič et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib43)): if dark matter is warm, it still needs to be relatively cold.

In practice, a suppressed power spectrum is used to re-create the initial conditions with the N-GenIC code (Springel et al., [2005](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib106); Angulo et al., [2012](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib3)). The difference between WDM and CDM and thus the amount of suppression can be fully modelled by a transfer function (Bode et al., [2001](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.12439v1#bib.bib13)):

### 7. COMPUTATIONAL TECHNIQUES

The process of galaxy formation involves nonlinear physics and a wide
variety of physical processes. As such, it is impossible to treat in
full detail using analytic techniques. There are two major approaches
that have been developed to circumvent this problem. The first,
numerical N-body simulation, attempts to directly and numerically solve
the fully nonlinear equations governing the physical processes inherent
to galaxy formation. The second, semi-analytic modeling, attempts to
construct a coherent set of analytic approximations which describe these
same physics. Each has its strengths and weaknesses, as will be
discussed below. As a result of this and of our ignorance of how some
key processes work, both approaches contain a little of each other
(rather like yin yang). A different, more empirical approach, utilizing
so-called "halo occupation distributions" has become widely used in the
past ten years and will be discussed briefly below.

**7.1. N-body/Hydro**

The most accurate computational method for solving the physics of galaxy
formation is via direct simulation, in which the fundamental equations
of gravitation, hydrodynamics and perhaps radiative cooling and transfer
are solved far a large number of points (arranged either on a grid or
following the trajectories of the fluid flow). I will not attempt to
review the numerical methods utilized in this approach in any detail
here (recent treatments of this can be found in
[Bertschinger 1998](Benson_refs.html#bertschinger_simulations_1998),
[Agertz et al. 2007](Benson_refs.html#agertz_fundamental_2007),
[Rosswog 2009](Benson_refs.html#rosswog_astrophysical_2009)),
but will instead merely highlight some of the approaches used.

Collisionless dark matter is (relatively) simple to model in this way,
since it responds only to the gravitational force. For the velocities
and gravitational fields occurring during structure and galaxy formation
non-relativistic Newtonian dynamics is more than adequate and so solving
the evolution of some initial distribution of dark matter (usually a
Gaussian random field of density perturbations consistent with the power
spectrum of the CMB) reduces to summing large numbers of 1 /
*r*2 forces between pairs of particles. In practice,
clever numerical techniques (such as particle-mesh, tree algorithms
etc.) are usually used to reduce this *N*2 problem into
something more manageable
([Kravtsov et al. 1997](Benson_refs.html#kravtsov_adaptive_1997);
[Springel 2005](Benson_refs.html#springel_cosmological_2005)).
The largest pure dark matter simulations carried out to date contain
around 10 billion particles
([Springel et al. 2005b](Benson_refs.html#springel_simulations_2005)).
Dark matter only simulations of this type (carried out primarily for the
cold dark matter scenario, but see
[White et
al. 1984](Benson_refs.html#white_size_1984),
[Klypin
et al. 1993](Benson_refs.html#klypin_structure_1993),
[Bode et
al. 2001](Benson_refs.html#bode_halo_2001),
[Davé et
al. 2001](Benson_refs.html#dave_halo_2001),
[Colín et al. 2002](Benson_refs.html#colin_structure_2002),
[Ahn and
Shapiro 2005](Benson_refs.html#ahn_formation_2005))
have been highly successful in determining the large scale structure of
the Universe, as embodied in the so-called "cosmic web". As a result,
the spatial and velocity correlation properties of dark matter and dark
matter halos
([Davis
et al. 1985](Benson_refs.html#davis_evolution_1985);
[White et
al. 1987a](Benson_refs.html#white_galaxy_1987),
[b](Benson_refs.html#white_clusters_1987);
[Efstathiou et al. 1988](Benson_refs.html#efstathiou_gravitational_1988);
[Eke et
al. 1996](Benson_refs.html#eke_cluster_1996);
[Jenkins et al. 1998](Benson_refs.html#jenkins_evolution_1998);
[Padilla
and Baugh 2002](Benson_refs.html#padilla_cluster_2002);
[Bahcall et al. 2004](Benson_refs.html#bahcall_evolution_2004);
[Kravtsov
et al. 2004](Benson_refs.html#kravtsov_dark_2004);
[Reed et
al. 2009](Benson_refs.html#reed_clustering_2009)),
together with the density profiles
([Navarro et al. 1997](Benson_refs.html#navarro_universal_1997);
[Bullock
et al. 2001b](Benson_refs.html#bullock_profiles_2001);
[Navarro et
al. 2004](Benson_refs.html#navarro_inner_2004);
[Merritt et al. 2005](Benson_refs.html#merritt_universal_2005);
[Prada et
al. 2006](Benson_refs.html#prada_far_2006)),
angular momenta
([Barnes
and Efstathiou 1987](Benson_refs.html#barnes_angular_1987);
[Efstathiou et al. 1988](Benson_refs.html#efstathiou_gravitational_1988);
[Warren et
al. 1992](Benson_refs.html#warren_dark_1992);
[Cole and
Lacey 1996](Benson_refs.html#cole_structure_1996);
[Lemson and Kauffmann 1999](Benson_refs.html#lemson_environmental_1999);
[Bullock et al. 2001a](Benson_refs.html#bullock_universal_2001);
[van den
Bosch et al. 2002](Benson_refs.html#bosch_angular_2002);
[Bett et
al. 2007](Benson_refs.html#bett_spin_2007);
[Gao et
al. 2008](Benson_refs.html#gao_redshift_2008))
and internal structure
([Moore et
al. 1999](Benson_refs.html#moore_dark_1999);
[Klypin
et al. 1999](Benson_refs.html#klypin_galaxies_1999);
[Kuhlen et
al. 2008](Benson_refs.html#kuhlen_via_2008);
[Springel et al. 2008](Benson_refs.html#springel_aquarius_2008))
of dark matter halos are known to very high accuracy.

Of course, to study galaxy formation dark matter alone is insufficient,
and baryonic material must be added in to the mix. This makes the
problem much more difficult since, at the very least, pressure forces
must be computed and the internal energy of the baryonic fluid
tracked. Particle-based methods (most prominently smoothed particle
hydrodynamics;
[Springel 2005](Benson_refs.html#springel_cosmological_2005))
have been successful in this area, as have Eulerian grid methods
([Ricker et al. 2000](Benson_refs.html#ricker_cosmos:hybrid_2000);
[Fryxell et al. 2000](Benson_refs.html#fryxell_flash:adaptive_2000);
[Plewa and Müller 2001](Benson_refs.html#plewa_amra:adaptive_2001);
[Quilis 2004](Benson_refs.html#quilis_new_2004)).
The addition of radiative cooling is relatively straightforward (at
least while the gas remains optically thin to its own radiation) by
simply tabulating the rate at which gas of given density and temperature
radiates energy.

Going beyond this level of detail becomes extremely
challenging. Numerous simulation codes are now able to include star
formation and feedback from supernovae explosions, while some even
attempt to follow the formation of supermassive black holes in galactic
centers. It should be kept in mind though that for galaxy scale
simulations the real physics of these processes is happening on scales
well below the resolution of the simulation and so the treatment of the
physics is often at the "subgrid" level, which essentially means that it
is put in by hand using a semi-analytic approach
([Thacker and Couchman 2000](Benson_refs.html#thacker_implementing_2000);
[Kay et
al. 2002](Benson_refs.html#kay_including_2002);
[Marri and
White 2003](Benson_refs.html#marri_smoothed_2003);
[White
2004](Benson_refs.html#white_feedback_2004);
[Stinson et
al. 2006](Benson_refs.html#stinson_star_2006);
[Cox et
al. 2006](Benson_refs.html#cox_feedback_2006);
[Scannapieco et al. 2006a](Benson_refs.html#scannapieco_feedback_2006);
[Tasker
and Bryan 2006](Benson_refs.html#tasker_simulating_2006);
[Stinson 2007](Benson_refs.html#stinson_supernova_2007);
[Vecchia and Schaye 2008](Benson_refs.html#vecchia_simulating_2008);
[Oppenheimer and Davé 2008](Benson_refs.html#oppenheimer_mass_2008);
[Okamoto
et al. 2008b](Benson_refs.html#okamoto_impact_2008);
[Booth
and Schaye 2009](Benson_refs.html#booth_cosmological_2009);
[Ciotti 2009](Benson_refs.html#ciotti_agn_2009)).

Beyond this, problems such as the inclusion of radiative transfer or
magnetic fields complicate the problem further by introducing new sets
of equations to be solved and the requirement to follow additional
fields. For example, in radiative transfer one must follow photons (or
photon packets) which have a position, direction of travel and
wavelength, and determine the absorption of these photons as they
traverse the baryonic material of the simulation, while simultaneously
accounting for the re-emission of absorbed photons at other
wavelengths. Despite these complexities, progress has been made on these
issues using a variety of ingenious numerical techniques
([Abel et al. 1999](Benson_refs.html#abel_photon-conserving_1999);
[Ciardi et al. 2001](Benson_refs.html#ciardi_cosmological_2001);
[Gnedin and Abel 2001](Benson_refs.html#gnedin_multi-dimensional_2001);
[Ricotti et
al. 2002](Benson_refs.html#ricotti_fate_2002);
[Petkova and Springel 2009](Benson_refs.html#petkova_implementation_2009);
[Li et al. 2008](Benson_refs.html#li_cosmomhd:cosmological_2008);
[Aubert
and Teyssier 2008](Benson_refs.html#aubert_radiative_2008);
[Collins et al. 2009](Benson_refs.html#collins_cosmological_2009);
[Dolag and
Stasyszyn 2008](Benson_refs.html#dolag_mhd_2008);
[Finlator et
al. 2009](Benson_refs.html#finlator_new_2009);
[Laursen et
al. 2009](Benson_refs.html#laursen_ly_2009)).
Providing Moore's Law continues to hold true, the ability of simulations
to provide ever more accurate pictures of galaxy formation should hold
strong. A brief survey of current cosmological hydrodynamical codes and
their functionalities is given in [Table 1](#Table 1).

**7.2. Semi-Analytic**

The technique of "semi-analytic modeling" or, perhaps, "phenomenological
galaxy formation modeling" takes the approach of treating the various
physical processes associated with galaxy formation using approximate,
analytic techniques. As with N-body/hydro simulations, the degree of
approximation varies considerably with the complexity of the physics
being treated, ranging from precision-calibrated estimates of dark
matter merger rates to empirically motivated scaling functions with
large parameter uncertainty (e.g. in the case of star formation and
feedback - just as in N-body/hydro simulations).

The primary advantage of the semi-analytic approach is that it is
computationally inexpensive compared to N-body/hydro simulations. This
facilitates the construction of samples of galaxies orders of magnitude
larger than possible with N-body techniques and for the rapid
exploration of parameter space
([Henriques et al. 2009](Benson_refs.html#henriques_monte_2009))
and model space (i.e. adding in new physics and assessing the
effects). The primary disadvantage is that they involve a larger degree
of approximation. The extent to which this actually matters has not yet
been well assessed. Comparison studies of semi-analytic vs. N-body/hydro
calculations have shown overall quite good agreement (at least on mass
scales well above the resolution limit of the simulation) but have been
limited to either simplified physics (e.g. hydrodynamics and cooling
only;
[Benson
et al. 2001b](Benson_refs.html#benson_comparison_2001),
[Yoshida et
al. 2002](Benson_refs.html#yoshida_gas_2002),
[Helly
et al. 2003b](Benson_refs.html#helly_comparison_2003)])
or to simulations of individual galaxies
([Stringer et al. 2010](Benson_refs.html#stringer_analytic_2010)).

Some of the earliest attempts to construct a self-consistent
semi-analytic model of galaxy formation began with
[White and
Frenk (1991)](Benson_refs.html#white_galaxy_1991),
[Cole
(1991)](Benson_refs.html#cole_modeling_1991)
and
[Lacey and
Silk (1991)](Benson_refs.html#lacey_tidally_1991),
drawing on earlier work by
[Rees and
Ostriker (1977)](Benson_refs.html#rees_cooling_1977)
and
[White and
Rees (1978)](Benson_refs.html#white_core_1978).
Since then numerous studies
([Kauffmann et al. 1993](Benson_refs.html#kauffmann_formation_1993);
[Baugh
et al. 1999b](Benson_refs.html#baugh_semianalytic_1999),
[1998](Benson_refs.html#baugh_epoch_1998);
[Somerville and Primack 1999](Benson_refs.html#somerville_semi-analytic_1999);
[Cole
et al. 2000](Benson_refs.html#cole_hierarchical_2000);
[Benson et
al. 2002a](Benson_refs.html#benson_effects_2002);
[Hatton et
al. 2003](Benson_refs.html#hatton_galics-_2003);
[Monaco et
al. 2007](Benson_refs.html#monaco_morgana_2007))
have extended and improved this original framework. A recent review of
semi-analytic techniques is given by
[Baugh (2006)](Benson_refs.html#baugh_primerhierarchical_2006).

**7.3. Halo Occupation Distributions**

Given the complexity of galaxy formation it is sometimes desirable to
take a more empirical approach to the problem. This can be advantageous
both to relate observations to the assumed underlying physical structure
of the Universe (e.g.
[Zheng et
al. 2007](Benson_refs.html#zheng_galaxy_2007),
[Tinker et al. 2010](Benson_refs.html#tinker_interpretingclustering_2010)),
and to make predictions based on extrapolations from current data and
which have a cosmological underpinning. "Halo occupation distributions",
first described by
[Neyman and
Scott (1952)](Benson_refs.html#neyman_theory_1952)
and used to study galaxy clustering (see, for example,
[Benson et
al. 2000c](Benson_refs.html#benson_nature_2000),
[Peacock and
Smith 2000](Benson_refs.html#peacock_halo_2000))
in their simplest form specify the probability of finding *N*
galaxies of some prescribed type in a dark matter halo of mass *M*,
*P*(*N*|*M*). Given this probability distribution,
knowledge of the distribution of dark matter halo masses and their
spatial distribution plus some assumptions about the locations of
galaxies within halos, one can construct (statistically or within an
N-body simulation) the distribution of galaxies. This allows, for
example, the abundance and clustering properties of those galaxies to be
inferred. A detailed discussion of the use of halo occupation
distributions in studying galaxy clustering is given by
[Cooray and
Sheth (2002)](Benson_refs.html#cooray_halo_2002).

This approach has recently been extended by incorporating simple
prescriptions relating, for example, star formation rates or quasar
activity to halo mass and redshift. Several authors have demonstrated
that such empirical models, while simple, can fit a wide variety of
galaxy data and can be used to gain insight into phenomena such as
"downsizing"
([Yang
et al. 2003](Benson_refs.html#yang_constraining_2003),
[Conroy
and Wechsler 2009](Benson_refs.html#conroy_connecting_2009),
[Croton
2009](Benson_refs.html#croton_simple_2009)).
At a fundamental level, the success of simple, empirical models such as
these suggests that, despite the complexity of galaxy formation physics,
its outcome is relatively simple.

### 7. COMPUTATIONAL TECHNIQUES

The process of galaxy formation involves nonlinear physics and a wide
variety of physical processes. As such, it is impossible to treat in
full detail using analytic techniques. There are two major approaches
that have been developed to circumvent this problem. The first,
numerical N-body simulation, attempts to directly and numerically solve
the fully nonlinear equations governing the physical processes inherent
to galaxy formation. The second, semi-analytic modeling, attempts to
construct a coherent set of analytic approximations which describe these
same physics. Each has its strengths and weaknesses, as will be
discussed below. As a result of this and of our ignorance of how some
key processes work, both approaches contain a little of each other
(rather like yin yang). A different, more empirical approach, utilizing
so-called "halo occupation distributions" has become widely used in the
past ten years and will be discussed briefly below.

**7.1. N-body/Hydro**

The most accurate computational method for solving the physics of galaxy
formation is via direct simulation, in which the fundamental equations
of gravitation, hydrodynamics and perhaps radiative cooling and transfer
are solved far a large number of points (arranged either on a grid or
following the trajectories of the fluid flow). I will not attempt to
review the numerical methods utilized in this approach in any detail
here (recent treatments of this can be found in
[Bertschinger 1998](Benson_refs.html#bertschinger_simulations_1998),
[Agertz et al. 2007](Benson_refs.html#agertz_fundamental_2007),
[Rosswog 2009](Benson_refs.html#rosswog_astrophysical_2009)),
but will instead merely highlight some of the approaches used.

Collisionless dark matter is (relatively) simple to model in this way,
since it responds only to the gravitational force. For the velocities
and gravitational fields occurring during structure and galaxy formation
non-relativistic Newtonian dynamics is more than adequate and so solving
the evolution of some initial distribution of dark matter (usually a
Gaussian random field of density perturbations consistent with the power
spectrum of the CMB) reduces to summing large numbers of 1 /
*r*2 forces between pairs of particles. In practice,
clever numerical techniques (such as particle-mesh, tree algorithms
etc.) are usually used to reduce this *N*2 problem into
something more manageable
([Kravtsov et al. 1997](Benson_refs.html#kravtsov_adaptive_1997);
[Springel 2005](Benson_refs.html#springel_cosmological_2005)).
The largest pure dark matter simulations carried out to date contain
around 10 billion particles
([Springel et al. 2005b](Benson_refs.html#springel_simulations_2005)).
Dark matter only simulations of this type (carried out primarily for the
cold dark matter scenario, but see
[White et
al. 1984](Benson_refs.html#white_size_1984),
[Klypin
et al. 1993](Benson_refs.html#klypin_structure_1993),
[Bode et
al. 2001](Benson_refs.html#bode_halo_2001),
[Davé et
al. 2001](Benson_refs.html#dave_halo_2001),
[Colín et al. 2002](Benson_refs.html#colin_structure_2002),
[Ahn and
Shapiro 2005](Benson_refs.html#ahn_formation_2005))
have been highly successful in determining the large scale structure of
the Universe, as embodied in the so-called "cosmic web". As a result,
the spatial and velocity correlation properties of dark matter and dark
matter halos
([Davis

## Introducing the AIDA-TNG project: Galaxy formation in alternative dark matter models

Giulia Despali1,2,3⋆, Lauro Moscardini1,2,3, Dylan Nelson4, Annalisa Pillepich5, Volker Springel6 and Mark Vogelsberger7

Abstract

We introduce the AIDA-TNG project, a suite of cosmological magnetohydrodynamic simulations that simultaneously model galaxy formation and different variations in the underlying dark matter model. We consider the standard cold dark matter model and five variations, including three warm dark matter scenarios and two self-interacting models with a constant or velocity-dependent cross-section. In each model, we simulated two cosmological boxes of 51.7 and 110.7 Mpc on a side with the same initial conditions as TNG50 and TNG100, and we combined the variations in the physics of dark matter with the fiducial IllustrisTNG galaxy formation model. The AIDA-TNG runs are thus ideal for studying the simultaneous effects of baryons and alternative dark matter models on observable properties of galaxies and large-scale structures. We resolved haloes in the range between 108 and 4×1014 M⊙ and scales down to the nominal resolution of 570 pc in the highest-resolution runs. This work presents the first results on statistical quantities such as the halo mass function and the matter power spectrum. We quantified the modification in the number of haloes and the power on scales smaller than 1 Mpc due to the combination of baryonic and dark matter physics. Despite being calibrated on cold dark matter, we find that the TNG galaxy formation model can produce a realistic galaxy population in all scenarios. The stellar and gas mass fraction, stellar mass function, black hole mass as a function of stellar mass, and star formation rate density are very similar in all dark matter models, with some deviations only in the most extreme warm dark matter model. Finally, we also quantify changes in halo structure due to warm and self-interacting dark matter, which appear in the density profiles, concentration-mass relation, and galaxy sizes.

Key words: methods: numerical / galaxies: evolution / dark matter / large-scale structure of Universe

## 1. Introduction

One of the foundations of the concordance cosmological model is that approximately 85% of the matter content of the Universe is in the form of some yet unknown component that can only be detected through its gravitational effect. Namely, this component is dark matter. Starting from small density perturbations in the early Universe, dark matter leads the gravitational collapse on large scales, forming the cosmic-web structure of filaments. Along these filaments, gas flows to the potential wells of dark matter haloes, where it cools and creates stars – eventually leading to the formation of galaxies. Despite decades of experimental searches, the favoured particle candidates for cold dark matter (CDM) – axions and weakly interacting massive particles (WIMPS) – have so far not been detected. This has motivated the study of alternative models that are allowed by particle physics assumptions and span a wide range of masses – from the electronvolt to the gigaelectron volt scale.

Moreover, while the CDM model is very successful at explaining the large-scale structure distribution of the Universe ([Planck Collaboration I 2014](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R83)), it has been challenged by observations at the scale of galaxies and below ([Bullock & Boylan-Kolchin 2017](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R19)). In the past few years, inconsistencies between CDM numerical simulations and observations that led to the famous ‘missing satellites problem’ ([Klypin et al. 1999](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R51)) have been mitigated with the inclusion of baryonic effects in simulations ([Brooks et al. 2013](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R17)) and with the discovery of new faint satellites in the Milky Way ([Bechtol et al. 2015](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R7); [Drlica-Wagner et al. 2015](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R31); [Kim et al. 2018](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R50)). However, other possible tensions have arisen, such as the ‘core-cusp’ and ‘too-big-to-fail’ problems ([Boylan-Kolchin et al. 2011](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R16)) or the diversity of rotation curves ([Kaplinghat et al. 2020](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R49)). Recent observational analyses have also identified new challenges for CDM. For example, the lensing properties of subhaloes appear inconsistent with CDM both at galaxy ([Minor et al. 2017](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R67); [Ballard et al. 2024](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R6); [Despali et al. 2024](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R29); [Enzi et al. 2024](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R35)) and cluster scales ([Meneghetti et al. 2020](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R65), [2023](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R66); [Ragagnin et al. 2022](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R86)). In both cases, the observed lensing signal is inferred to be stronger than predicted, and the subhalo concentrations required to explain it are outliers within the CDM model. It is thus still uncertain if simulated CDM predictions can be brought completely into agreement with observations by modifications of baryonic feedback and other aspects of the subgrid physics in simulations or if these discrepancies point towards new physics in the dark sector.

Many previous works have addressed the aforementioned tensions, with some developing a theoretical and computational framework especially suited for warm dark matter (WDM) and self-interacting dark matter (SIDM). These models were originally motivated by models of the satellite population of galaxies (for WDM), the profiles of dwarf galaxies, and merging clusters (for SIDM). While these effects are certainly among the most relevant, the most stringent constraints on the WDM particle mass are derived from observations of the Lyman-*α* forest ([Iršič et al. 2024](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R46)) and flux-ratio anomalies of lensed quasars ([Gilman et al. 2020a](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R40)), while the self-interaction cross-section is strongly constrained at the scale of massive elliptical galaxies and galaxy clusters ([Meneghetti et al. 2001](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R64); [Peter et al. 2013](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R79); [Despali et al. 2019](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R26), [2022](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R28); [Eckert et al. 2022](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R32); [Shen et al. 2022](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R100); [Mastromarino et al. 2023](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R63)). In general, any dark matter model that aspires to explain observations should be challenged with reproducing the halo and galaxy properties spanning many decades in mass and not only in a narrow range of scales. This is the fundamental aim of the AIDA-TNG project.

The majority of the work in the field of alternative dark matter is based on zoom-in simulations of limited samples of objects ([Zavala et al. 2013](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R125); [Rocha et al. 2013](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R92); [Fry et al. 2015](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R39); [Robles et al. 2017](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R91); [Creasey et al. 2017](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R23); [Despali et al. 2019](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R26), [2022](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R28); [Bhattacharyya et al. 2022](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R12); [Nadler et al. 2023](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R70); [O’Neil et al. 2023](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R77); [Ragagnin et al. 2024](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R87)). These simulations can have the advantage of high spatial and mass resolution, but they lack the required statistics to test models on multiple scales and fully understand potential biases in the sample selection. At the same time, the inclusion of baryonic physics is fundamental for studying small scales, but only a limited number of available alternative dark matter (ADM) hydrodynamical simulations are of cosmological scale (see Fig. [1](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#F1)). For WDM, [Oman et al. (2024)](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R76) recently presented two cosmological runs that use the Eagle galaxy formation model: one 25 Mpc box where the WDM model considers a 1.5 keV thermal relic candidate and one 34 Mpc box reproducing the effect of 7.1 keV sterile neutrinos. In SIDM, the EAGLE-SIDM ([Robertson et al. 2018](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R88), [2021](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R90); [Forouhar Moreno et al. 2022](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R38)) and BAHAMAS-SIDM ([Robertson et al. 2019](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R89)) simulations have been the first to include both self-interactions and baryonic physics on cosmological scales. This set includes elastic self-interactions with constant cross-sections *σ*/*m**χ*=[0.1, 1] cm2 g−1 and a model with a mild velocity dependence peaking at *σ*/*m**χ* = 3 cm2 g−1. More recently, the TANGO-SIDM simulations ([Correa et al. 2022](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R21), [2024](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R22)) explored velocity-dependent SIDM models in 25 Mpc volumes, including the SWIFT-EAGLE physics model ([Schaller et al. 2024](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R96); [Bahé et al. 2022](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R5); [Borrow et al. 2023](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R14)). Finally, the THESAN-HR ([Shen et al. 2024](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R101)) simulations include different dark matter variations; however, given their focus on the epoch of reionisation, they only follow a ∼6 Mpc box until *z* = 6.

The present work introduces the 50 and 100 Mpc AIDA runs, while the highest-resolution 20 Mpc box will be presented in an upcoming paper.

The AIDA-TNG project advances the field of ADM simulations by providing a consistent set of high-resolution cosmological boxes simulated with the same galaxy formation model and multiple WDM and SIDM models, in addition to CDM. We provide a large simulated sample of galaxies and break the degeneracy between baryonic and dark matter effects at small scales. In this work, we present simulations evolving two cosmological volumes of 51.7 and 110.7 Mpc on a side evolved with the AREPO code ([Springel 2010](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R105)) in different dark matter scenarios and in the TNG galaxy formation model ([Weinberger et al. 2017](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R120); [Pillepich et al. 2018a](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R80)).

We present the first results from the AIDA-TNG suite, providing an overview of the multiple effects of ADM models. In Sect. [2](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#S2), we describe the set-up of the AIDA simulations and discuss the considered dark matter models and their properties. We then present our main results in Sects. [3](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#S6) to [6](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#S11). We start by discussing the halo and stellar mass function in Sect. [3](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#S6) and their evolution with redshift. Section [4](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#S7) focuses on the effects of ADM on the distribution of dark matter and baryons within haloes, exploring the density profiles and concentration-mass relation. We then look at the properties of galaxies in Sect. [5](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#S10). Finally, Sect. [6](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#S11) presents measurements of the matter power spectra of the different mass components and their evolution in time. In Sect. [7](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#S14), we summarise our results and present our conclusions.

## 2. The AIDA simulations

The AIDA-TNG simulations, *Alternative Dark Matter in the TNG universe* (AIDA for short, hereafter), consist of three cosmological volumes of increasing resolution and decreasing size simulated in cold and ADM models, with and without baryonic physics. In this work we present the two largest boxes of 110.7 and 51.7 Mpc on a side and the first results obtained from their analysis, while a smaller (and higher resolution) 20 Mpc box will be introduced in an upcoming work. The uniqueness of the AIDA set lies in the fact that we run the same cosmological volumes in multiple dark matter scenarios, spanning cold, warm and self-interacting dark matter. Moreover, we create both a dark matter-only (DMO) and a full-physics (FP) version of each run so that we can disentangle the effects of ADM and baryonic physics as described by the IllustrisTNG galaxy formation model (TNG hereafter; [Weinberger et al. 2017](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R120); [Pillepich et al. 2018a](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R80)). We discuss the considered dark matter scenarios and the integration with the TNG model in more detail in Sects. [2.1, 2.2](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#S6%20S7) and [2.3](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#S5).

All simulations start at redshift *z* = 127 and adopt the cosmological model from [Planck Collaboration XXIV (2016)](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R84): Ωm = 0.3089, ΩΛ = 0.6911, Ωb = 0.0486, *H*0 = 0.6774 and *σ*8 = 0.8159. The initial conditions (ICs) have been generated with N-GENIC code ([Springel 2005](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R104)) by applying the Zel’dovich approximation on a glass distribution of particles with a linear matter transfer function computed using the CAMB code ([Lewis et al. 2000](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R54)). The 110.7 and 51.7 Mpc boxes (corresponding to 75 and 35 *h*−1Mpc, respectively) are run from the same ICs of the corresponding runs of the original IllustrisTNG simulations ([Pillepich et al. 2018b](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R81)). For each case, we simulate two resolution levels: ‘A’ corresponds to the TNG100-2 and TNG50-2 runs, while ‘B’ corresponds to the TNG100-3 and TNG50-3 runs. Therefore, we can compare to many previous results on galaxy formation in CDM and better understand systematics. Haloes and subhaloes are identified using the SUBFIND algorithm, producing catalogues of the halo and galaxy properties with the same information available for the TNG runs ([Nelson et al. 2019](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R74)). In the FP 50/A runs, we reach a best spatial resolution of *ϵ*DM, \* = 570 parsecs (gravitational softening length), as well as a dark matter and target baryon mass resolution of mDM = 3.6×106 M⊙ and mbar = 6.8×105 M⊙, respectively.

Table [1](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#T1) lists the properties of all other runs, and which combinations of box size, resolution and dark matter model have been computed. The choice was made with the aim of a economic use of computational resources and is based on which models produce significant differences compared to CDM at a given resolution – an issue particularly relevant for the WDM models, as discussed in the next section.

### 2.1. Warm dark matter models

In WDM models, dark matter particles are lighter (keV) than CDM candidates (GeV) and thus become non-relativistic at sufficiently late times so as to suppress, by free streaming, the formation of low-mass galactic-scale haloes with respect to CDM. The number of satellites of the Milky Way and the properties of its stellar streams, strong gravitational lensing and the Lyman-*α* forest are all well-known probes of the WDM particle mass *m*WDM. Each of these is sensitive to the number of low-mass haloes and subhaloes in certain redshift and halo mass ranges: our Galaxy, gravitational lenses at distances 0.1≤*z*lens≤3, and the distribution of neutral gas on large scales. Joint constraints that combine the results of such methods are thus more robust than individual measurements. Very warm models are currently already excluded, with upper limits at mWDM≥5 keV ([Gilman et al. 2020b](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R41)), mWDM≥6.046 keV ([Enzi et al. 2021](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R34)), mWDM≥7.4 keV ([Nadler et al. 2021](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R69)), mWDM≥4.1 keV ([Iršič et al. 2024](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R46)): If dark matter is warm, it still needs to be relatively cold.

In practice, a suppressed power spectrum is used to re-create the initial conditions with the N-GENIC code ([Springel et al. 2005](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R107); [Angulo et al. 2012](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R3)). The difference between WDM and CDM, and thus the amount of suppression, can be fully modelled by a transfer function ([Bode et al. 2001](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R13)):

The parameter *α* characterises the scale of damping and *ν* = 1.2, *g**X* = 1.5 ([Viel et al. 2005](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R114)). In this way, small-scale fluctuations (high *k*) are erased while the power spectrum is identical to the CDM one on large scales. From the ratio of the WDM and CDM *P*(*k*), one can define the half-mode mass *M*hm that corresponds to the scale at which the power spectrum is suppressed by one half and is thus the halo mass below which we expect to erase almost all structures. In the AIDA sample, we consider three different WDM models where the mass of the dark matter particle candidates is mWDM=(1, 3, 5) keV, corresponding to the half-mode masses Mhm=(1.26×1010, 3.26×108, 5.95×107) *h*−1 M⊙. The left panel of Fig. [2](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#F2) shows the initial power spectra of the considered models. In comparison to the existing observational limits:

* –

  The warmest model (WDM1 – mWDM = 1 keV) is already ruled out since it is incompatible with structure formation constraints. However, we use it as an extreme case to emphasise any differences with respect to the CDM simulations and to test the effects of baryonic physics in such a different scenario. We do not run this model at the highest resolution given that it erases most structures with masses Mvir∼1010 M⊙ (see Sect. [3](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#S6)). These are already well sampled by the mass resolution of the 100/B and 50/B boxes (see Table [1](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#T1)) which are sufficient to study it properly.
* –

  The intermediate scenario (WDM3 – mWDM = 3 keV), is somewhat at the edge of observational constraints: it is already ruled out by combined analysis, while some individual probes (for example gravitational lensing) still allow it. Given the lack of cosmological hydrodynamical simulations with this model, most constraints are based on predictions from DMO runs. We may not yet fully understand possible systematics coming from zoom-in runs of small samples of galaxies and the extent of possible deviations from CDM at all scales, making it an interesting scenario. Moreover, this model (together with its 7 keV sterile-neutrino counterpart) has been used in the majority of recent numerical explorations of WDM effects ([Lovell et al. 2012](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R57), [2014](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R58); [Oman et al. 2024](/articles/aa/full_html/2025/05/aa53836-25/aa53836-25.html#R76)), and thus it is useful for comparisons to previous work.
* –
